topics.title: Number System
topics.subtitle: Classification, Divisibility, Factors, Remainders, Cyclicity & More

readingSections.title: The Number Family Tree (Classification)
readingSections.content: Numbers are grouped into families based on their properties. Each family builds on the previous one, like nesting dolls.

subsections.title: Natural Numbers (N)
subsections.content: Counting numbers starting from 1: $1, 2, 3, 4, \ldots$

subsections.title: Whole Numbers (W)
subsections.content: Natural numbers plus zero: $0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$. Every natural number is also a whole number.

subsections.title: Integers (Z)
subsections.content: Whole numbers and their negatives: $\ldots, -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$. Used for temperature, debt, elevation.

subsections.title: Rational Numbers (Q)
subsections.content: Any number written as $p/q$ with $q \neq 0$. Includes integers, fractions, terminating decimals ($0.75$), repeating decimals ($0.\overline{3}$). All integers are rational ($5 = 5/1$).

subsections.title: Irrational Numbers
subsections.content: Cannot be written as $p/q$. Decimal never ends and never repeats. Examples: $\sqrt{2} = 1.414\ldots$, $\pi = 3.14159\ldots$, $e = 2.71828\ldots$

subsections.title: Real Numbers (R)
subsections.content: Every rational and irrational number together. Every point on the number line is a real number. This is what we use in everyday math.

readingSections.title: The Building Blocks (Primes & Composites)
readingSections.content: Think of primes as the atoms of the number world. Every number can be broken down into primes, just like everything is made of atoms.

subsections.title: Prime Numbers
subsections.content: A prime has exactly 2 factors: 1 and itself. Primes: $2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23, \ldots$. 2 is the only even prime. A number ending in 5 (except 5 itself) is never prime.

subsections.title: Composite Numbers
subsections.content: More than 2 factors. Composites: $4, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12, \ldots$. Every composite can be broken into smaller primes. Example: $12 = 2 \times 2 \times 3$.

subsections.title: Co-Prime Numbers
subsections.content: Two numbers are co-prime when their HCF (GCD) is 1. They dont need to be prime themselves. Examples: $(8,15)$ has HCF 1. Any two consecutive numbers are always co-prime, like $(14,15)$.

subsections.title: Checking if a Number is Prime
subsections.content: Step 1: Find $\sqrt{n}$. Step 2: Check divisibility by primes up to $\sqrt{n}$. Step 3: If none divide evenly, $n$ is prime. Example: Check 97. $\sqrt{97} \approx 9.8$. Check 2, 3, 5, 7. None divide 97. So 97 is prime.

readingSections.title: Speed Math (Divisibility Rules)
readingSections.content: Divisibility rules let you check division without actually dividing. Master these for lightning-fast calculations in exams.

subsections.title: By 2
subsections.content: Last digit is even: $0, 2, 4, 6, 8$. Example: 124 ends in 4, so divisible by 2.

subsections.title: By 3
subsections.content: Sum of digits divisible by 3. Example: 123 has sum $1+2+3=6$, 6 is divisible by 3, so 123 is divisible by 3.

subsections.title: By 4
subsections.content: Last 2 digits divisible by 4. Example: 1324 has last 2 digits 24, and $24 \div 4 = 6$, so divisible by 4.

subsections.title: By 5
subsections.content: Last digit is $0$ or $5$. Example: 125 ends in 5, divisible by 5.

subsections.title: By 6
subsections.content: Divisible by BOTH 2 and 3. Example: 126 is even (by 2) and $1+2+3=6$ (by 3), so divisible by 6.

subsections.title: By 8
subsections.content: Last 3 digits divisible by 8. Example: 1232 has last 3 digits 232, and $232 \div 8 = 29$, so divisible by 8.

subsections.title: By 9
subsections.content: Sum of digits divisible by 9. Example: 261 has sum $2+6+1=9$, 9 is divisible by 9.

subsections.title: By 10
subsections.content: Last digit is $0$. Example: 150 ends in 0, divisible by 10.

subsections.title: By 11
subsections.content: Difference of sums of alternate digits is $0$ or multiple of 11. Example: 121: sum of odd positions $1+1=2$, even positions $=2$. Difference $= 0$. So 121 is divisible by 11.

readingSections.title: HCF & LCM Made Easy
readingSections.content: HCF finds the biggest common divisor. LCM finds the smallest common multiple. They are two sides of the same coin.

subsections.title: Finding HCF (GCD)
subsections.content: Step 1: Prime factorize each number. Step 2: Pick common primes with LOWEST powers. Step 3: Multiply them. Example: HCF of 24 ($2^3 \times 3$) and 36 ($2^2 \times 3^2$). Common primes: 2 and 3. Lowest powers: $2^2$ and $3^1$. HCF $= 4 \times 3 = 12$.

subsections.title: Finding LCM
subsections.content: Step 1: Prime factorize each number. Step 2: Pick ALL primes with HIGHEST powers. Step 3: Multiply them. Example: LCM of 24 ($2^3 \times 3$) and 36 ($2^2 \times 3^2$). Pick $2^3$ and $3^2$. LCM $= 8 \times 9 = 72$.

subsections.title: The Golden Formula
subsections.content: For any two numbers $a$ and $b$: $a \times b = \text{HCF}(a,b) \times \text{LCM}(a,b)$. If you know any three values, find the fourth. Example: If product is 288 and HCF is 12, then LCM $= 288 \div 12 = 24$.

readingSections.title: The Last Digit Trick (Cyclicity)
readingSections.content: The unit digit of powers follows a repeating pattern called cyclicity. This is one of the most tested concepts in placement exams.

subsections.title: Always Same (Cyclicity 1)
subsections.content: Digits 0, 1, 5, 6: These never change. $5^{100}$ ends in 5. $6^{50}$ ends in 6. $1^{1000}$ ends in 1.

subsections.title: Alternating (Cyclicity 2)
subsections.content: Digits 4 and 9 switch between two values. For 4: $4^{\text{odd}} = 4$, $4^{\text{even}} = 6$. For 9: $9^{\text{odd}} = 9$, $9^{\text{even}} = 1$.

subsections.title: How to Solve Any Cyclicity Problem
subsections.content: Step 1: Take the unit digit of the base number. Step 2: Find its cycle length (1, 2, or 4). Step 3: Divide the exponent by the cycle length. Step 4: Use the remainder to pick from the cycle. Remainder 0 means the last element. Example: Unit digit of $7^{345}$. Cycle of 7: $[7,9,3,1]$, length 4. $345 \div 4 = 86$ remainder 1. 1st element is 7. Answer: 7.

readingSections.title: Remainder Theorems (Level-Up Shortcuts)
readingSections.content: Master these theorems to solve remainder problems in seconds. Each theorem has a specific trigger that tells you when to use it.

subsections.title: Basic Algebraic Remainder
subsections.content: Trigger: Expression like $(ax + b)^n$ divided by $a$. Shortcut: Ignore the $ax$ term, just compute $b^n \div a$. Example: Remainder of $10^{20} \div 9$. Write $10 = 9+1$. Then $(9+1)^{20}$: every term except $1^{20}$ has factor 9. Remainder $= 1^{20} = 1$.

subsections.title: Fermat's Little Theorem
subsections.content: Trigger: Large exponent with a PRIME modulus. Theorem: If $p$ is prime and $a$ not divisible by $p$, then $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$. Example: $3^{100} \bmod 7$. Since 7 is prime, $3^6 \equiv 1 \pmod{7}$. $100 = 6 \times 16 + 4$, so $3^{100} \equiv 3^4 = 81 \equiv 4 \pmod{7}$.

subsections.title: Euler's Theorem
subsections.content: Trigger: Large exponent with a COMPOSITE modulus (and base and modulus are co-prime). Theorem: If $\gcd(a,n) = 1$, then $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$. Example: $2^{100} \bmod 15$. $\gcd(2,15)=1$. $\phi(15) = 15(1-1/3)(1-1/5) = 8$. $2^8 \equiv 1 \pmod{15}$. $100 = 8\times12+4$, so $2^{100} \equiv 2^4 = 16 \equiv 1 \pmod{15}$.

subsections.title: Chinese Remainder Theorem
subsections.content: Trigger: A number has different remainders with different divisors. Problem: Find $x$ such that $x \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$ and $x \equiv 2 \pmod{5}$. Step 1: For small numbers, just try values. Numbers $\equiv 1$ mod 3: 1, 4, 7, 10, 13, 16... Check mod 5: 1$\to$1 (no), 4$\to$4 (no), 7$\to$2 (yes!). Answer: $x = 7$. The general solution is $x = 7 + 15k$ (since $3 \times 5 = 15$).

formulas.title: Sum of First n Natural Numbers
formulas.explanation: The sum of the first $n$ natural numbers $1+2+3+\cdots+n$ equals $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$. This is derived from pairing first and last terms: $(1+n)+(2+(n-1))+\cdots$

formulas.title: Sum of First n Odd Numbers
formulas.explanation: The sum of the first $n$ odd numbers $1+3+5+\cdots+(2n-1)$ equals $n^2$.

formulas.title: Sum of First n Even Numbers
formulas.explanation: The sum of the first $n$ even numbers $2+4+6+\cdots+2n$ equals $n(n+1) = n^2 + n$.

formulas.title: Sum of Squares of First n Natural Numbers
Sum of Cubes of First n Natural Numbers
formulas.explanation: The sum $1^3 + 2^3 + 3^3 + \cdots + n^3 = \left[\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\right]^2$. This is the square of the sum of natural numbers.

formulas.title: Product of Two Numbers
formulas.explanation: For any two positive integers $a$ and $b$, the product of the numbers equals the product of their HCF and LCM.

formulas.title: Number of Factors
formulas.explanation: If $N = a^{p_1} \times b^{p_2} \times c^{p_3} \times \cdots$ where $a,b,c$ are primes, then the number of factors is the product of $(\text{exponent}+1)$ for each prime.

formulas.title: Sum of All Factors
formulas.explanation: The sum of all factors uses the geometric series formula for each prime factor. For $N = a^p$, the sum of its powers $1+a+\cdots+a^p = \frac{a^{p+1}-1}{a-1}$.

formulas.title: Fermat's Little Theorem
formulas.explanation: If $p$ is a prime number and $a$ is not divisible by $p$, then $a^{p-1}$ leaves remainder $1$ when divided by $p$. This is crucial for solving large exponent remainder problems.

formulas.title: Euler's Theorem
formulas.explanation: If $\gcd(a,n) = 1$, then $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ where $\phi(n) = n\prod_{p|n}\left(1-\frac{1}{p}\right)$. This generalizes Fermat's theorem.

formulas.title: Cyclicity of Unit Digits
formulas.formula: \text{Unit digit of } a^n \text{ repeats every } k \text{ powers}
formulas.explanation: The unit digit follows a cycle: 0,1,5,6 have $k=1$; 4,9 have $k=2$; 2,3,7,8 have $k=4$. Algorithm: $\text{unit}(a^n) = \text{cycle}[n \bmod k]$ where index $0$ = last element.
formulas.example: Find unit digit of $7^{345}$. Cycle of 7: $[7,9,3,1]$, $k=4$. $345 \bmod 4 = 1$. 1st element $= 7$.

formulas.title: Trailing Zeros in Factorial
formulas.explanation: Trailing zeros count the number of factors of $10 = 2\times5$. Since there are always more factors of $2$ than $5$, count factors of $5$ in $n!$.

formulas.title: Digital Root
formulas.explanation: Digital root is the repeated sum of digits until a single digit. It equals $N \bmod 9$, with $9$ representing $0$. Perfect squares can only have DR $1,4,7,9$.

formulas.title: Last Two Digits
formulas.explanation: For numbers ending in 1, the last two digits follow this pattern. For numbers ending in 5: $(10a+5)^2 = 100a(a+1)+25$, so last two digits are always $25$. For other endings, use CRT with mod $4$ and mod $25$.
formulas.example: $31^{24}$: $10\times3\times24+1 = 721 \to$ last two digits $= 21$. $85^2$: last two digits $= 25$.

formulas.title: Binomial Expansion for Remainders
formulas.explanation: For remainder problems like $(a+1)^n \div a$, expand binomially. All terms except $1^n$ contain factor $a$, so remainder $= 1^n = 1$. More generally, $(am+b)^n \div a$ gives remainder $b^n \div a$.
formulas.example: Find remainder of $10^{20} \div 9$. $10 = 9+1$, so $10^{20} = (9+1)^{20}$. All terms except $1^{20}$ have factor $9$. Remainder $= 1$.

formulas.title: Chinese Remainder Theorem
formulas.explanation: Given $x \equiv r_i \pmod{m_i}$ with $\gcd(m_i,m_j)=1$, let $M = \prod m_i$, $M_i = M/m_i$, and $y_i$ be the modular inverse of $M_i$ modulo $m_i$ (i.e., $M_i y_i \equiv 1 \pmod{m_i}$). Then the unique solution modulo $M$ is $x \equiv \sum r_i M_i y_i \pmod{M}$.

formulas.title: Product of All Factors
formulas.explanation: If $N = a^{p_1} \times b^{p_2} \times c^{p_3} \times \cdots$, then the product of all factors of $N$ equals $N$ raised to half the number of factors. If $d(N)$ is the number of factors, then product $= N^{d(N)/2}$. Factors pair up as $f \times N/f$ giving product $N$ per pair.

formulas.title: Number of Odd & Even Factors
formulas.explanation: If $N = 2^{p_1} \times a^{p_2} \times b^{p_3} \times \cdots$ where $a,b$ are odd primes, then: (1) Odd factors come only from the odd primes, ignoring the power of 2. (2) Even factors = Total factors - Odd factors. The number of odd factors is $(p_2+1)(p_3+1)\cdots$.

formulas.title: Least Number to Multiply or Divide for Perfect Square
formulas.formula: \text{Multiply/Divide so each prime exponent becomes even}
formulas.explanation: To make a number a perfect square: prime factorize, then for each prime with an odd exponent, multiply by that prime (to make exponent even) or divide by that prime (to remove odd power). The least number to multiply is the product of all primes with odd exponents. The least number to divide is the product of all primes with odd exponents (removing one power of each).
formulas.example: $72 = 2^3 \times 3^2$. Exponents: $2^3$ has odd exponent 3, $3^2$ has even exponent 2. To make perfect square: multiply by $2$ to get $2^4 \times 3^2 = 144 = 12^2$. Or divide by $2$ to get $2^2 \times 3^2 = 36 = 6^2$. Least multiplier $= 2$, least divisor $= 2$.

formulas.title: Number of Ways as Product of Two Factors
formulas.formula: \text{Ways} = \begin{cases} \frac{d(N)}{2} & \text{if } N \text{ is not a perfect square} \\ \frac{d(N)+1}{2} & \text{if } N \text{ is a perfect square} \end{cases}
formulas.explanation: The number of ways to write $N$ as a product of two factors (order not considered, i.e., $a \times b$ and $b \times a$ count as one). Factors pair up as $f$ and $N/f$. If $N$ is a perfect square, the square root pairs with itself, giving one extra unpaired factor. Number of ordered pairs = $d(N)$ (each factor pairs with its complement).
formulas.example: $36 = 2^2 \times 3^2$, $d(N)=9$. Since $36$ is a perfect square, ways $= (9+1)/2 = 5$. The pairs: $1\times36, 2\times18, 3\times12, 4\times9, 6\times6$.

formulas.title: Checking if a Number is Prime
formulas.explanation: To check if a number $n$ is prime: (1) Check if $n \leq 1$ (not prime). (2) Check if $n$ is 2 or 3 (prime). (3) Check if $n$ is even or divisible by 3. (4) For $i = 5$ to $\sqrt{n}$, check if $n$ is divisible by $i$ or $i+2$. If none divide, $n$ is prime. Since all primes $> 3$ are of form $6k \pm 1$, we only need to check divisors of the form $6k \pm 1$.
formulas.example: Check if 97 is prime. $\sqrt{97} \approx 9.8$. Check divisibility by 2,3,5,7. 97 mod 2 = 1, mod 3 = 1, mod 5 = 2, mod 7 = 6. None divide evenly, so 97 is prime.

formulas.title: Digital Root for Perfect Square Verification
formulas.explanation: A perfect square can only have digital root 1, 4, 7, or 9. If a number has digital root 2, 3, 5, 6, or 8, it CANNOT be a perfect square. This is a quick elimination test. Reason: squares modulo 9 are only 0,1,4,7. Since digital root = n mod 9 (with 0 mapped to 9), the possible digital roots of squares are 1,4,7,9.
formulas.example: Check if 12345 can be a perfect square. DR of 12345: $1+2+3+4+5=15$, $1+5=6$. DR = 6, which is not in {1,4,7,9}. Therefore 12345 is NOT a perfect square.

formulas.title: Last Two Digits for Numbers Ending in 5
formulas.formula: (10a+5)^n \text{ ends with } \begin{cases} 25 & \text{if } n \geq 2 \\ 75 & \text{if } a \text{ is odd and } n=1 \text{ or sometimes } 25 \end{cases}
formulas.explanation: For a number ending in 5: $(10a+5)^1$ ends in 25 if a is even, 75 if a is odd. $(10a+5)^2 = 100a(a+1)+25$, which always ends in 25. For $n \geq 2$, the last two digits are always 25 regardless of a. This is because the square always contributes a factor of 25, and all higher powers preserve this.
formulas.example: $85^2: a=8$ (even). $100\times8\times9+25 = 7225$, last two digits 25. $75^2: a=7$ (odd). $100\times7\times8+25 = 5625$, last two digits still 25. For $n \geq 2$, answer is always 25.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is a prime number?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Check each number. 21 = 3x7 (composite). 23 has factors only 1 and 23 (prime). 27 = 3^3 (composite). 33 = 3x11 (composite). Step 2: Therefore, 23 is the only prime number.

mcqs.q: What is the smallest prime number?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: By definition, a prime number has exactly two factors: 1 and itself. Step 2: 0 is not prime (undefined). 1 has only one factor (itself), so it is neither prime nor composite. Step 3: 2 has factors 1 and 2 - it is the smallest and only even prime. Step 4: Answer is 2.

mcqs.q: Which is the only even prime number?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: All even numbers except 2 are divisible by 2 (so they have at least 3 factors). Step 2: 2 = 2x1, it has exactly 2 factors. Step 3: 4 = 2x2, 6 = 2x3, 8 = 2x4 - all composite. Step 4: Answer is 2.

mcqs.q: How many prime numbers are between 1 and 20?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: List primes: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19. Step 2: Count: 8 numbers. Step 3: Note: 1 is not prime, 4,6,8,9,10,12,14,15,16,18,20 are composite.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is a composite number?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Check each. 2 is prime (factors: 1,2). 3 is prime (factors: 1,3). 4 = 2x2 (factors: 1,2,4) - composite. 5 is prime. Step 2: Answer is 4.

mcqs.q: The number 0 belongs to which set?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Natural numbers start from 1. Step 2: Whole numbers = N + {0}, so 0 is a whole number. Step 3: 0 is not prime (undefined for numbers < 2). Step 4: 0 is rational (0 = 0/1). Step 5: Answer is Whole numbers.

mcqs.q: What type of number is sqrt(2)?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: sqrt(2) cannot be expressed as p/q where p,q are integers (q != 0). Step 2: Its decimal expansion 1.414213... is non-terminating and non-repeating. Step 3: Therefore, sqrt(2) is irrational.

mcqs.q: What is the smallest prime number?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: By definition, a prime number has exactly two factors: 1 and itself. Step 2: 0 is not prime (undefined). 1 has only one factor (itself), so it is neither prime nor composite. Step 3: 2 has factors 1 and 2 - it is the smallest and only even prime. Step 4: Answer is 2.

mcqs.q: Which is the only even prime number?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: All even numbers except 2 are divisible by 2 (so they have at least 3 factors). Step 2: 2 = 2x1, it has exactly 2 factors. Step 3: 4 = 2x2, 6 = 2x3, 8 = 2x4 - all composite. Step 4: Answer is 2.

mcqs.q: How many prime numbers are between 1 and 20?

mcqs.q: Which of the following is a composite number?

mcqs.q: The number 0 belongs to which set?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Natural numbers start from 1. Step 2: Whole numbers = N + {0}, so 0 is a whole number. Step 3: 0 is not prime (undefined for numbers < 2). Step 4: 0 is rational (0 = 0/1). Step 5: Answer is Whole numbers.

mcqs.q: What type of number is sqrt(2)?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: sqrt(2) cannot be expressed as p/q where p,q are integers (q != 0). Step 2: Its decimal expansion 1.414213... is non-terminating and non-repeating. Step 3: Therefore, sqrt(2) is irrational.

mcqs.q: Which set includes all positive integers?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Positive integers = 1,2,3,... These are exactly the natural numbers. Step 2: Whole numbers include 0. Integers include 0 and negatives. Rational includes fractions. Step 3: Answer is Natural numbers.

mcqs.q: What is the sum of first 10 natural numbers?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Use formula S = n(n+1)/2. Step 2: n = 10. Step 3: S = 10x11/2 = 110/2 = 55. Step 4: Answer is 55.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is a rational number?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Rational = can be expressed as p/q. Step 2: sqrt(2) and sqrt(3) are irrational. pi is irrational. Step 3: 22/7 = 3.142857142857... (repeating decimal) - this IS rational because it is a ratio of two integers. Step 4: Answer is 22/7.

mcqs.q: Two numbers are co-prime if their HCF is:
mcqs.exp: Step 1: By definition, co-prime numbers have HCF = 1. Step 2: Example: 8 and 15: factors of 8 are 1,2,4,8; factors of 15 are 1,3,5,15. Common: only 1. Step 3: So HCF = 1, meaning they are co-prime.

mcqs.q: Which is the smallest composite number?

mcqs.exp: Step 1: By definition, a prime number has exactly two factors: 1 and itself. Step 2: 0 is not prime (undefined). 1 has only one factor (itself), so it is neither prime nor composite. Step 3: 2 has factors 1 and 2 - it is the smallest and only even prime. Step 4: Answer is 2.

mcqs.exp: Step 1: 117: sum=9, divisible by 3 and 9 (117=9x13). Step 2: 119: 119/7=17, composite (7x17). Step 3: 121=11^2, composite. Step 4: 127: check primes up to sqrt(127)~11.3: not divisible by 2,3,5,7,11. 127 mod 7=1, mod 11=6. Therefore 127 is prime.

mcqs.q: Find the least number to multiply 54 to make it a perfect square.
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 54 = 2 x 3^3. Step 2: Exponents: 2^1 (odd), 3^3 (odd). Step 3: Multiply by 2 x 3 = 6 to get 2^2 x 3^4 = 324 = 18^2. Step 4: Answer is 6.

mcqs.q: How many ordered pairs of coprime factors does 60 have?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 60 = 2^2 x 3 x 5. Each prime goes entirely into one factor. Step 2: 3 primes, each can go to factor A or factor B: 2^3 = 8 ordered pairs. Step 3: But (1,60) and (60,1) etc. 8 ordered pairs. Verify: (1,60), (4,15), (3,20), (5,12), (12,5), (20,3), (15,4), (60,1). Step 4: Answer is 8.

mcqs.q: Which of these is divisible by 7?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 210 = 7 x 30, directly divisible by 7. Step 2: 205/7 = 29r2. 207/7 = 29r4. 209/7 = 29r6. Step 3: Only 210 is divisible by 7.

mcqs.q: How many prime numbers are between 50 and 60?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Numbers 51-59. Step 2: 51=3x17, 52 even, 53 prime, 54 even, 55=5x11, 56 even, 57=3x19, 58 even, 59 prime. Step 3: Primes: 53, 59. Count = 2.

mcqs.q: How many even factors does 48 have?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 48 = 2^4 x 3. Total factors = (4+1)(1+1)=10. Step 2: Odd factors: only from 3^1, so (1+1)=2 (1 and 3). Step 3: Even factors = 10-2 = 8.

mcqs.q: Which number is definitely NOT a perfect square?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Check unit digits: 9,4,1,2. A square cannot end in 2. Step 2: 1562 ends in 2, so it cannot be a perfect square. Step 3: Verify: 37^2=1369, 38^2=1444, 39^2=1521.
Step 1: 2^50: cycle [2,4,8,6], 50 mod 4 = 2, unit digit = 4. Step 2: 3^50: cycle [3,9,7,1], 50 mod 4 = 2, unit digit = 9. Step 3: 4+9=13, unit digit = 3.

Step 1: sqrt(233) ~ 15.3. Step 2: Check 2: odd, not divisible. 3: 2+3+3=8, not divisible. 5: not ending in 0/5. 7: 7x33=231, 233 mod 7=2. 11: 11x21=231, remainder 2. 13: 13x17=221, 233-221=12. Step 3: None divide, so 233 is prime.

mcqs.q: Find the number of odd factors of 200.
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 200 = 2^3 x 5^2. Step 2: Odd factors: ignore 2^3, only 5^2 contributes. Step 3: Odd factors = 2+1 = 3. (1,5,25).

mcqs.q: Find the least number to multiply 98 to make it a perfect square.
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 98 = 2 x 7^2. Step 2: Exponents: 2^1 (odd), 7^2 (even). Step 3: Multiply by 2 to get 2^2 x 7^2 = 196 = 14^2.

mcqs.q: Product of all factors of N is N^8. How many factors does N have?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Product = N^{d(N)/2} = N^8. Step 2: d(N)/2 = 8, so d(N) = 16. Step 3: N has 16 factors.
Step 1: Base unit digit = 7. Cycle: [7,9,3,1], length 4. Step 2: 100 mod 4 = 0. Step 3: Remainder 0 = 4th element in cycle = 1.

mcqs.q: Find x such that x=2(mod3) and x=3(mod5).
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Numbers = 2 (mod 3): 2,5,8,11,14,17,... Step 2: Check which = 3 (mod 5): 2/5=2 (no), 5/5=0 (no), 8/5=3 (yes!). Step 3: x=8. Check: 8=2x3+2, 8=1x5+3. Correct.

Step 1: 100! has 24 trailing zeros. Step 2: 200! has 49 trailing zeros. Step 3: The sum has as many zeros as the smaller factorial: 24. Step 4: Since 200! ends with 49 zeros and 100! ends with 24, the sum ends with 24 zeros (the 25th digit from right will be non-zero in 100! but zero in 200!; adding them gives non-zero). Actually more precisely: Z(min(100!,200!)) = 24.

Step 1: 7^7 = 823543. But we only need its mod 4. Step 2: 7 mod 4 = 3. Step 3: So exponent = 7^7 = 3 (mod 4). Step 4: Cycle of 7: [7,9,3,1], position = 3 mod 4 = 3rd element = 3. Wait: 7 mod 4 = 3. So 7^7 mod 4 = 3^7 mod 4. Step 5: 3^1=3, 3^2=9=1, 3^3=3, 3^4=1. Cycle of 3 mod 4: [3,1], length 2. Step 6: 7 mod 2 = 1, so 3^7 mod 4 = 3. Step 7: Now 7^x where x=3 (mod 4). Cycle of 7: [7,9,3,1], position 3 = 3rd = 3. Answer: 3.

mcqs.q: Find the remainder when 2^90 is divided by 25.
mcqs.exp: Step 1: By Eulers theorem, phi(25)=20, so 2^20 = 1 (mod 25). Step 2: 90 = 20x4 + 10. Step 3: 2^90 = 2^10 = 1024 (mod 25). Step 4: 1024 = 25x40 + 24. Remainder = 24.

mcqs.q: Find the smallest number that when divided by 3,4,5 leaves remainder 2.
mcqs.exp: Step 1: LCM of 3,4,5 = 60. Step 2: Number = 60k + 2. Step 3: Smallest = 60+2 = 62. Check: 62/3=20r2, /4=15r2, /5=12r2.

mcqs.q: How many numbers from 1 to 200 are divisible by 2 or 3?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Divisible by 2: floor(200/2)=100. Step 2: Divisible by 3: floor(200/3)=66. Step 3: Both: floor(200/6)=33. Step 4: Total = 100+66-33 = 133.

mcqs.q: What is the sum of first 20 even numbers?
The HCF of two numbers is 12 and product is 720. Find their LCM.
mcqs.exp: Step 1: a x b = HCF x LCM. Step 2: 720 = 12 x LCM. Step 3: LCM = 720/12 = 60.
Step 1: Base ends in 6. Step 2: 6 has cyclicity 1 (always ends in 6). Step 3: 2026^2026 ends in 6.

mcqs.q: When 10^20 is divided by 9, the remainder is:
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 10 = 9+1. Step 2: 10^20 = (9+1)^20. Step 3: Binomial expansion: all terms except 1^20 have factor 9. Step 4: Remainder = 1^20 = 1.

mcqs.q: Find the number of factors of 144.
If x%4 = 2 and x%6 = 4, find the smallest positive x.
mcqs.exp: Step 1: From x%4=2: x = 2,6,10,14,18,22,... Step 2: Check against x%6=4: 2%6=2(no), 6%6=0(no), 10%6=4(yes!). Step 3: Smallest x = 10. Check: 10/4=2r2, 10/6=1r4. Correct.

mcqs.q: How many factors of 60 are odd?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 60 = 2^2 x 3 x 5. Step 2: Odd factors come from ignoring 2^2: 3^1 x 5^1. Step 3: Odd factors = (1+1)(1+1) = 4. Step 4: Odd factors: 1,3,5,15.

topics.title: Simplification & Approximation
topics.subtitle: BODMAS, Surds & Indices, Square/Cube Roots, Approximation, Vedic Math & More

readingSections.title: The BODMAS / VBODMAS Rule
readingSections.content: When you see a long expression with mixed operators (+, -, *, /) and brackets ( ), { }, [ ], and the word 'of', use BODMAS. This is the #1 most tested rule in TCS, Infosys, Wipro, and Cognizant placement papers. Companies love to place 'of' right before multiplication to trap students. Remember: 'Of' comes BEFORE Division and Multiplication.

subsections.title: The Order (VBODMAS)
subsections.content: Step 1: V = Vinculum (bar bracket $\overline{a-b}$). Solve this FIRST. Step 2: B = Brackets ( ), { }, [ ] from inner to outer. Step 3: O = Of (fractions/percentages or literal 'of'). Step 4: D = Division. Step 5: M = Multiplication. Step 6: A = Addition. Step 7: S = Subtraction. Note: Division/Multiplication have same priority, solve left to right. Same for Addition/Subtraction.

subsections.title: Pattern Recognition - How to Spot BODMAS Questions
subsections.content: Pattern: A long string of numbers with multiple operators like $12 + 8 \div 4 \times 3 - 5$. Trap Pattern: 'Of' next to division, like $24 \div 6 \text{ of } 2 + 5$. If you do division first, you get the wrong answer! Always do 'Of' first.

subsections.title: Common Mistakes in Placement Exams
subsections.content: Mistake 1: Doing division before 'of'. Rule: 'Of' is like a hidden multiplication that must be solved first. Mistake 2: Solving addition before subtraction when subtraction comes first (left to right rule). Mistake 3: Forgetting vinculum/bar bracket which has highest priority. Example: $\overline{9-3}$ must be solved before anything else.

subsections.title: Step-by-Step BODMAS Problem Solving
subsections.content: Problem: $12 + 8 \div 4 \times 3 - 5$. Step 1 (Division): $8 \div 4 = 2$, expression: $12 + 2 \times 3 - 5$. Step 2 (Multiplication): $2 \times 3 = 6$, expression: $12 + 6 - 5$. Step 3 (Add/Sub left to right): $12 + 6 = 18$, $18 - 5 = 13$. Answer: 13.

readingSections.title: Surds & Indices (Laws of Exponents)
readingSections.content: Indices questions appear in almost every placement paper. The key pattern: variables in the exponent position like $2^x = 32$, or fractional powers like $27^{2/3}$. The golden rule: Convert everything to the SAME BASE. If you cannot, use the laws of exponents.

subsections.title: The 6 Core Laws of Indices
subsections.content: Law 1: $a^m \times a^n = a^{m+n}$ (Product: add powers). Law 2: $a^m \div a^n = a^{m-n}$ (Quotient: subtract powers). Law 3: $(a^m)^n = a^{mn}$ (Power of power: multiply). Law 4: $a^0 = 1$ (any non-zero base). Law 5: $a^{-m} = 1/a^m$ (negative power = reciprocal). Law 6: $a^{m/n} = (\sqrt[n]{a})^m$ (fractional power = root then power).

subsections.title: Pattern Recognition for Indices
subsections.content: Pattern 1: Same base different exponents $\rightarrow$ use Law 1 or 2. Pattern 2: Different base same exponent $\rightarrow$ try to convert to same base. Pattern 3: Fractional exponents $\rightarrow$ use Law 6 (root-power). Pattern 4: Negative exponents $\rightarrow$ use Law 5 (reciprocal). Pattern 5: Complex expression like $(x^a/x^b)^{a^2+ab+b^2} \times ... $ $\rightarrow$ use Law 3 and algebraic identities.

readingSections.title: Square Roots & Cube Roots (Speed Shortcuts)
readingSections.content: When asked to find $\sqrt{X}$ or $\sqrt[3]{X}$ where X is a 4 to 6 digit perfect square/cube, NEVER use long division. Use the unit-digit shortcut method. This saves 30+ seconds per question.

subsections.title: Square Root Unit Digit Shortcut
subsections.content: Memorize: Ends in 1 $\rightarrow$ root ends in 1 or 9. Ends in 4 $\rightarrow$ root ends in 2 or 8. Ends in 5 $\rightarrow$ root ends in 5. Ends in 6 $\rightarrow$ root ends in 4 or 6. Ends in 9 $\rightarrow$ root ends in 3 or 7. Ends in 0 $\rightarrow$ root ends in 0. Ends in 2,3,7,8 $\rightarrow$ NOT a perfect square! To find tens digit: ignore last 2 digits, find which perfect squares the remaining part falls between, pick the smaller number.

subsections.title: Cube Root Unit Digit Shortcut
subsections.content: Memorize: 1$\to$1, 2$\to$8, 3$\to$7, 4$\to$4, 5$\to$5, 6$\to$6, 7$\to$3, 8$\to$2, 9$\to$9, 0$\to$0. To find tens digit: group digits in threes from right, ignore last group, find cube of remaining part.

subsections.title: The Check Trick for Square Roots
subsections.content: After finding tens digit (say T), compute $T \times (T+1)$. If remaining part (ignored last 2 digits) is greater than this product, pick the larger unit digit. If smaller, pick the smaller unit digit. Example: $\sqrt{5776}$: tens digit=7, $7\times8=56$. Remaining=57. 57>56, so pick larger unit digit 6. Answer: 76.

readingSections.title: Approximation & Fraction-Percentage Master Table
readingSections.content: When the question says 'What approximate value should come in place of the question mark?' or 'What is the approximate value of...', DO NOT calculate exactly. Round off numbers to nearest integers or simple multiples. The Fraction-to-Percentage table is your secret weapon.

subsections.title: Rounding Rules for Approximation
subsections.content: Rule 1: Round 19.02 to 19, 4.99 to 5. Rule 2: 34.95% round to 35%. Rule 3: $\sqrt{624.9}$ round to $\sqrt{625}=25$. Rule 4: 44.99 ÷ 4.99 round to 45 ÷ 5 = 9. Rule 5: 3.01% of 500 = 3% of 500 = 15.

subsections.title: Pattern Recognition for Approximation
subsections.content: If numbers look like 19.99, 34.95%, 4.98, $\sqrt{624.9}$: you MUST approximate. If the question says 'approximate value' or 'closest value': never solve exactly. If the options are far apart (like 380, 385, 390, 395), rough approximation works.

readingSections.title: Vedic Math & Fast Calculation Shortcuts
readingSections.content: These are the speed boosters that separate top scorers from the rest. Master these two shortcuts: squaring numbers ending in 5, and multiplying numbers near a base (100, 1000, etc.).

subsections.title: Shortcut 1: Squaring Numbers Ending in 5
subsections.content: Formula: $(X5)^2 = X \times (X+1) \text{ followed by } 25$. Example: $75^2$: $7 \times 8 = 56$, append 25 $\rightarrow$ 5625. Works for any number ending in 5: $35^2 = 1225$, $85^2 = 7225$, $105^2 = 11025$, $125^2 = 15625$.

subsections.title: Shortcut 2: Multiplication Near Base 100
subsections.content: Formula for numbers ABOVE base: $(100+a) \times (100+b) = (100+a+b) \times 100 + (a \times b)$. Example: $104 \times 107$: surpluses +4 and +7. Cross add: $104+7=111$ (left part). Multiply: $4\times7=28$ (right part). Answer: 11128. For BELOW base: $96 \times 97$: deficiencies -4 and -3. Cross subtract: $96-3=93$ (left part). Multiply: $(-4)\times(-3)=12$ (right part). Answer: 9312.

formulas.title: BODMAS Order of Operations
formulas.formula: \text{V} \to \text{B} \to \text{O} \to \text{D/M} \to \text{A/S} \quad (\text{left to right within D/M and A/S})
formulas.explanation: The VBODMAS rule defines the priority order: Vinculum (bar bracket) first, then Brackets from inner to outer, then Of, then Division and Multiplication (equal priority, left to right), then Addition and Subtraction (equal priority, left to right). This is the foundation of all simplification.

formulas.title: The 'Of' Rule in BODMAS
formulas.formula: a \text{ of } b = a \times b \quad \text{(solved before other D/M)}
formulas.explanation: 'Of' acts like multiplication but has HIGHER priority than regular division and multiplication. This is the most common trap in placement exams. Whenever you see 'of', solve that multiplication FIRST before any other division or multiplication in the expression.
formulas.example: $24\div6\text{ of }2+5$: Step 1: $6\text{ of }2=12$ (O). Step 2: $24\div12=2$ (D). Step 3: $2+5=7$ (A). Answer: 7. (Wrong method: $24\div6=4$, $4\times2=8$, $8+5=13$.)

formulas.title: Vinculum / Bar Bracket Rule
formulas.explanation: Vinculum (the bar over an expression) has the HIGHEST priority in VBODMAS. Solve everything under the bar before solving any other brackets. This is often tested in TCS and Infosys exams with nested brackets.

formulas.title: Law of Indices: Multiplication
formulas.explanation: When multiplying terms with the SAME base, ADD the exponents. This is the most frequently used law. Applied when you see expressions like $5^x \times 5^y$ or $256^{0.16} \times 256^{0.09}$.

formulas.title: Law of Indices: Division
formulas.explanation: When dividing terms with the SAME base, SUBTRACT the exponents (numerator exponent minus denominator exponent). Used for expressions like $5^{x+3}/5^{x-1}$ or $(x^a)/(x^b)$.

formulas.title: Law of Indices: Power of a Power
formulas.explanation: When a power is raised to another power, MULTIPLY the exponents. Used for complex expressions like $(x^a/x^b)^{a^2+ab+b^2}$ or $(27)^{2/3}$.

formulas.title: Negative & Fractional Indices
formulas.explanation: Negative exponent means reciprocal. Fractional exponent means root then power. Example: $2^{-3}=1/8$. $8^{2/3}=(\sqrt[3]{8})^2=2^2=4$.

formulas.title: Square Root Unit Digit Shortcut
formulas.explanation: Never use long division. Step 1: Check unit digit of number to find possible root endings. Step 2: Ignore last 2 digits, find the perfect square below the remaining number (this gives tens digit). Step 3: Use the check: tens digit x (tens digit+1). If remainder > this, pick larger unit digit.
formulas.example: $\sqrt{1849}$: Ends in 9, so root ends in 3 or 7. Ignore 49, remaining 18. $4^2=16$, $5^2=25$, so tens digit=4. $4\times5=20$. 18<20, so pick smaller unit digit 3. Answer: 43.

formulas.title: Cube Root Unit Digit Shortcut
formulas.formula: \text{If } X \text{ ends in } d, \text{ root ends in: } 1\to1, 2\to8, 3\to7, 4\to4, 5\to5, 6\to6, 7\to3, 8\to2, 9\to9, 0\to0
formulas.explanation: Step 1: Use unit digit mapping to find unit digit of root. Step 2: Group digits in threes from right, ignore last 3 digits. Step 3: Find the cube that is below or equal to the remaining number - that's the tens digit.
formulas.example: $\sqrt[3]{13824}$: Ends in 4, so root ends in 4. Groups: 13|824, remaining=13. $2^3=8$, $3^3=27$, so tens digit=2. Combine: 24. Answer: 24.

formulas.title: Fraction to Percentage Conversion Table
formulas.explanation: The fraction-to-percentage table is your secret weapon for approximation. Convert complex percentages instantly: $14.28\%=1/7$, $33.33\%=1/3$, $12.5\%=1/8$, etc. This turns percentage calculations into simple division.

formulas.title: Approximation & Rounding Rules
formulas.explanation: When you see 'approximate value' or numbers like 19.99, 4.98, $\sqrt{624.9}$, $44.99\%$: round to the nearest convenient number. The goal is to make calculation trivial without significant error. Apply BODMAS on rounded values.
formulas.example: $19.02\times4.99\times X=380$: Step 1: $19.02\approx19$, $4.99\approx5$. Step 2: $19\times5\times X=380 \to 95X=380$. Step 3: $X=380/95=4$. Answer: 4.

formulas.title: Squaring Numbers Ending in 5
formulas.explanation: For any number ending in 5: multiply the tens digit by the next consecutive number, then append 25. This is 10x faster than traditional multiplication.
formulas.example: $75^2$: Step 1: Tens digit X=7. Step 2: $7\times8=56$. Step 3: Append 25. Answer: 5625. Check: $75\times75=5625$.

formulas.title: Multiplication Near Base 100/1000
formulas.explanation: For numbers near 100: find surpluses/deficiencies. Cross add/subtract for left part. Multiply surpluses/deficiencies for right part. Right part must have 2 digits for base 100 (carry if needed), 3 digits for base 1000.
formulas.example: $104\times107$: Surpluses +4,+7. Left: $104+7=111$. Right: $4\times7=28$. Answer: 11128. $96\times97$: Deficiencies -4,-3. Left: $96-3=93$. Right: $(-4)\times(-3)=12$. Answer: 9312.

topics.title: Analogies
topics.subtitle: Word, Letter, Number Analogies & Relationship Patterns

readingSections.title: What is an Analogy?
readingSections.content: An analogy is a comparison between two things that shows how they are similar. Companies use analogy questions to test your reasoning, vocabulary, and pattern recognition skills. These appear in TCS, Infosys, Wipro, Accenture and almost all campus placement exams.

subsections.title: Why Companies Ask Analogy Questions
subsections.content: Companies want people who think logically and spot patterns quickly. Solving analogies proves you can: (1) Understand relationships between concepts, (2) Think abstractly, (3) Make quick decisions under pressure. These are essential IT job skills.

subsections.title: Basic Structure
subsections.content: An analogy looks like: A : B :: C : ? (read as A is to B as C is to what). Your job is to find the relationship between A and B, then apply it to C. Example: Doctor : Hospital :: Teacher : ? Answer: School (a doctor works in a hospital, a teacher works in a school).

subsections.title: Real-Life Example
subsections.content: If your friend says My phone is to me what wings are to a bird, you understand the phone is essential for communication just like wings are essential for flight. You are comparing two relationships that work the same way.

subsections.title: How to Approach
subsections.content: Step 1: Read the first pair carefully. Step 2: Identify the exact relationship. Step 3: Apply the same relationship to C. Step 4: Find the matching answer. Step 5: Double-check by reversing the logic.

readingSections.title: Word Analogies (Most Common)
readingSections.content: Word analogies use regular English words. The relationship can be based on meaning, function, category, or association.

subsections.title: Synonym Analogies (Same Meaning)
subsections.content: The relationship is same meaning. Example: Happy : Joyful :: Sad : ? Answer: Unhappy. Common pairs: Big-Large, Small-Tiny, Fast-Quick, Brave-Courageous, Afraid-Scared.

subsections.title: Antonym Analogies (Opposite Meaning)
subsections.content: The relationship is opposite meaning. Example: Hot : Cold :: Light : ? Answer: Dark. Common pairs: Up-Down, Good-Bad, Happy-Sad, Rich-Poor, Strong-Weak.

subsections.title: Cause and Effect Analogies
subsections.content: One word causes the other. Example: Rain : Flood :: Virus : ? Answer: Disease. Ask yourself: Does A cause B?

subsections.title: Function/Use Analogies
subsections.content: Shows what something is used for. Example: Pen : Write :: Knife : ? Answer: Cut. Pattern: A is used to B.

subsections.title: Part to Whole Analogies
subsections.content: First word is part of the second. Example: Finger : Hand :: Toe : ? Answer: Foot. More: Page : Book, Wheel : Car, Leaf : Tree.

subsections.title: Profession and Tool
subsections.content: Shows what tool a professional uses. Example: Doctor : Stethoscope :: Carpenter : ? Answer: Hammer. Common: Teacher-Chalk, Chef-Knife, Painter-Brush.

subsections.title: Animal and Young Ones
subsections.content: Shows parent and baby relationship. Example: Cat : Kitten :: Dog : ? Answer: Puppy. Memorize: Cow-Calf, Lion-Cub, Sheep-Lamb, Horse-Foal.

subsections.title: Animal and Home/Dwelling
subsections.content: Shows where an animal lives. Example: Dog : Kennel :: Bird : ? Answer: Nest. Learn: Lion-Den, Rabbit-Burrow, Bee-Hive, Horse-Stable.

subsections.title: Country and Capital
subsections.content: Shows capital cities. Example: India : Delhi :: USA : ? Answer: Washington DC. Must-know: India-Delhi, UK-London, France-Paris, Japan-Tokyo.

subsections.title: Person and Achievement
subsections.content: Shows who created something. Example: Shakespeare : Hamlet :: Einstein : ? Answer: Theory of Relativity. Common: Newton-Gravity, Edison-Light Bulb.

readingSections.title: Letter and Number Analogies
readingSections.content: Letter analogies use alphabet patterns and number analogies use mathematical relationships.

subsections.title: Position-Based Letter Analogies
subsections.content: Uses A=1, B=2, C=3...Z=26. Example: A : D :: B : ? Answer: E (A+3=D, B+3=E). Common: +1, +2, +3 shifts, reverse positions, mirror positions.

subsections.title: Skip Pattern Analogies
subsections.content: Letters skip a fixed number. Example: A : C :: E : ? Answer: G (skip 1 letter).

subsections.title: Mirror/Reverse Analogies
subsections.content: A <-> Z, B <-> Y, C <-> X. Example: ABC : ZYX :: DEF : ? Answer: WVU.

subsections.title: Addition/Subtraction Number Patterns
subsections.content: Simple arithmetic. Example: 5 : 8 :: 10 : ? Answer: 13 (add 3).

subsections.title: Multiplication and Square Patterns
subsections.content: Example: 4 : 12 :: 6 : ? Answer: 18 (multiply by 3). Or 3 : 9 :: 5 : ? Answer: 25 (square).

subsections.title: Digit-Based Patterns
subsections.content: Manipulates digits. Example: 12 : 3 :: 45 : ? Answer: 9 (1+2=3, 4+5=9). Or reverse digits.

readingSections.title: Pattern Recognition - Find Relationships Fast
readingSections.content: The key to solving analogies quickly is recognizing the relationship type within 10-15 seconds.

subsections.title: The 5-Second Classification Test
subsections.content: Ask in order: (1) Are they synonyms? (2) Are they opposites? (3) Does one cause the other? (4) Is one part of the other? (5) Is there a functional relationship? Practice until automatic.

subsections.title: Keyword Triggers
subsections.content: Similar meanings -> Synonym. Opposite meanings -> Antonym. Action words -> Function. Place names -> Location. Person names -> Achievement. Numbers -> Mathematical. Animals -> Young One or Home.

subsections.title: Sentence Frame Technique
subsections.content: Force into a sentence: A is a type of B, A uses B, A lives in B, A causes B, A is opposite of B. The frame that fits tells you the relationship.

subsections.title: Elimination Strategy
subsections.content: When stuck: (1) Cross out options that don't match word type. (2) Eliminate reversed relationships. (3) Remove overly specific or general answers. Usually 2-3 options get eliminated quickly.

subsections.title: Common Traps
subsections.content: Trap 1: Reversed relationship. Trap 2: Partial match. Trap 3: Association without true relationship. Trap 4: Too specific. Always verify: Does C relate to my answer exactly like A relates to B?

readingSections.title: Company-Specific Patterns
readingSections.content: Different companies favor different analogy types. Knowing their patterns gives you an edge.

subsections.title: TCS Patterns
subsections.content: TCS heavily tests: Profession-Tool, Animal-Young, Country-Capital, Synonym-Antonym mix. Difficulty: Medium. Time: 45 seconds per question.

subsections.title: Infosys Patterns
subsections.content: Infosys prefers: Cause-Effect, Part-Whole, Letter analogies with complex skips, Function-based. Difficulty: Medium-Hard.

subsections.title: Wipro Patterns
subsections.content: Wipro focuses on: Synonym-Antonym, Number analogies with squares/cubes, Simple function. Difficulty: Easy-Medium.

subsections.title: Accenture/Capgemini
subsections.content: Accenture: Mix of word and letter analogies, emphasis on speed. Capgemini: Loves classification analogies.

subsections.title: Cognizant/HCL
subsections.content: Cognizant: Straightforward word analogies, synonyms/antonyms. HCL: More abstract relationships and GK-based analogies.

readingSections.title: Speed Shortcuts and Memory Tricks
readingSections.content: Master these to solve analogies in 20-30 seconds.

subsections.title: The 3-Second Relationship Label
subsections.content: The moment you read A:B, mentally say: Opposites!, Same!, Tool!, Home!, Baby!, Capital!. This labels the relationship instantly and prevents overthinking.

subsections.title: EJOTY Trick for Letter Positions
subsections.content: Remember E=5, J=10, O=15, T=20, Y=25. Use these anchors to find positions quickly. M is 2 before O(15) so M=13.

subsections.title: Memory Tricks for Capitals
subsections.content: Create silly sentences: Delhi is India capital, DC is USA capital, London has Queen, Paris has Eiffel Tower. Weird associations are memorable.

subsections.title: Reverse Check Shortcut
subsections.content: After picking your answer, quickly verify by reversing: If A:B::C:D works, then B:A::D:C should also make sense. If not, reconsider!

subsections.title: Animal Young Grouping
subsections.content: Group by pattern: Cub endings for big cats/bears, Calf for large animals (cow, elephant, whale), Lamb for sheep, Foal for horses, Chick for birds.

formulas.title: Synonym Relationship Rule
formulas.explanation: When A and B have similar meanings, find a word with similar meaning to C.

formulas.title: Antonym Relationship Rule
formulas.formula: A = opposite of B => C = opposite of ?
formulas.explanation: When A and B are opposites, find the opposite of C.

formulas.title: Cause and Effect Rule
formulas.explanation: When A causes B, find what C causes.

formulas.title: Function/Use Rule
formulas.formula: A is used to B => C is used to ?
formulas.explanation: When B is the function of A, find the function of C.

formulas.title: Part to Whole Rule
formulas.formula: A is part of B => C is part of ?
formulas.explanation: When A is part of B, find what C is part of.

formulas.title: Profession and Tool Rule
formulas.explanation: When B is the tool of profession A, find the tool of profession C.

formulas.title: Animal and Young One Rule
formulas.explanation: When B is the baby of animal A, find the baby of animal C.

formulas.title: Animal and Dwelling Rule
formulas.formula: A lives in B => C lives in ?
formulas.explanation: When B is the home of animal A, find the home of animal C.

formulas.title: Country and Capital Rule
formulas.explanation: When B is capital of country A, find capital of country C.

formulas.title: Letter Shift Formula
formulas.explanation: When B is n positions after A in alphabet, find n positions after C.

formulas.title: Letter Mirror/Reverse Rule
formulas.explanation: Position p maps to position (27-p).

formulas.title: Number Square Rule
formulas.explanation: When second number is square of first.

formulas.title: Digit Sum Rule
formulas.explanation: Sum the digits of the number.

topics.title: Coding-Decoding
topics.subtitle: Letter Shifts, Number Coding, Mirror Coding & Substitution

readingSections.title: What is Coding-Decoding?
readingSections.content: Coding-Decoding is a reasoning skill where letters, numbers, or symbols are transformed using a hidden rule. Your task is to crack the logic. This is one of the most scoring topics in campus placement exams like TCS, Infosys, Wipro, and Accenture.

subsections.title: Why Companies Ask
subsections.content: Coding-decoding tests your ability to find patterns quickly. In IT jobs, you constantly understand how data transforms from one form to another. This shows recruiters you can: find hidden rules, apply rules consistently, and work systematically under pressure.

subsections.title: Basic Structure
subsections.content: You get a word and its code. Example: CAT is coded as FDW. Find the pattern, then either code another word or decode a given code. All questions follow: find the rule, apply the rule.

subsections.title: Real-Life Example
subsections.content: Think of a secret language where every letter shifts by +3 (A->D, B->E). When friend sends FDW, you decode it as CAT. Companies test how fast you learn the secret language.

subsections.title: Success Strategy
subsections.content: Step 1: Write A=1 to Z=26 on rough paper. Step 2: Compare each original letter with its code. Step 3: Note the difference per position. Step 4: Apply same pattern to new word. Step 5: Verify at least 2 letters before finalizing.

readingSections.title: Letter Coding Types
readingSections.content: Letter coding transforms letters into other letters. This is the most common category.

subsections.title: Forward Shift Coding
subsections.content: Each letter moves forward by fixed number. Example: CAT->FDW (+3 each). C+3=F, A+3=D, T+3=W. Common shifts: +1, +2, +3, +5. If wrapping: Z+1=A (circular alphabet).

subsections.title: Backward Shift Coding
subsections.content: Each letter moves backward. Example: DOG->ALD (-3). D-3=A, O-3=L, G-3=D. For wrapping: A-1=Z.

subsections.title: Mirror/Reverse Coding
subsections.content: A<->Z, B<->Y, C<->X. Formula: new_pos = 27 - old_pos. A(1) becomes Z(26). This is like seeing the alphabet in a mirror.

subsections.title: Alternating Pattern Coding
subsections.content: Different letters get different shifts. Example: LAP->OBS: L+3=O, A+1=B, P+3=S. Always check 2+ letter pairs to identify the alternating rule.

subsections.title: Group Coding
subsections.content: Groups of letters coded together. Example: AB->CD, EF->GH. Or reverse pairs: AB->BA, CD->DC.

readingSections.title: Number and Mixed Coding
readingSections.content: Numbers represent letters through position values. Letters and numbers can mix.

subsections.title: Direct Position Coding
subsections.content: Write the position number: A=1, B=2... CAT = 3-1-20 = 3120. Very common in TCS and Infosys.

subsections.title: Sum/Product of Positions
subsections.content: Positions are added or multiplied. CAT = 3+1+20 = 24 (single number). Check if code length matches word (concatenated) or shorter (sum).

subsections.title: Digit Manipulation
subsections.content: Multiple operations on digits: sum (12->3), reverse (23->32), product (12->2).

subsections.title: Symbol-Based Coding
subsections.content: Symbols like star, circle, #, $ represent letters/numbers. Straightforward substitution.

subsections.title: Mixed Letter-Number Coding
subsections.content: Code has both letters and numbers. Example: GO = G(7)+O(15) = 715. Just concatenate positions.

readingSections.title: Advanced and Company Patterns
readingSections.content: Placement exams often combine multiple patterns.

subsections.title: Conditional Coding
subsections.content: Rule changes based on position or letter type. Vowels get +2, consonants get +3. Or first letter gets +1, last +2, middle +3.

subsections.title: Vowel-Consonant Coding
subsections.content: Different rules for vowels (A,E,I,O,U) and consonants. Identify vowels first, then apply rules.

subsections.title: TCS Coding Patterns
subsections.content: Forward/backward shifts (+3, +5), Mirror coding, Position value concatenation, Alternating patterns. 45 sec per question.

subsections.title: Infosys Coding Patterns
subsections.content: Complex alternating patterns, Vowel-consonant coding, Mixed letter-number. 60 sec per question. Harder.

subsections.title: Wipro/Accenture Patterns
subsections.content: Simple shifts (+1,+2,+3), Direct substitution, Position coding. Easier but faster pace: 30-35 sec.

readingSections.title: Speed Shortcuts
readingSections.content: Solve coding-decoding in 15-25 seconds.

subsections.title: EJOTY Trick
subsections.content: E=5, J=10, O=15, T=20, Y=25. Use nearest anchor. Need S? S is 1 before T(20)=19. Need G? G is 2 after E(5)=7.

subsections.title: The 27-Minus Trick
subsections.content: For mirror coding: 27 - position. Memorize one formula. A(1) -> 27-1=26=Z. M(13) -> 27-13=14=N.

subsections.title: Pattern Detection Speed
subsections.content: Check first AND last letter pairs first. If same shift, rule is uniform. If different, check for alternating.

subsections.title: Two-Letter Verification
subsections.content: Never guess from one pair. Always check at least two letter pairs. If they match, you are confident.

subsections.title: Reverse Check for Decoding
subsections.content: To decode, reverse the rule. If coding was +3, decoding is -3. Mirror (27-pos) is its own reverse!

readingSections.title: Common Mistakes
readingSections.content: Learn these to avoid losing easy marks.

subsections.title: Wrong Direction
subsections.content: If coding adds +3, decoding subtracts -3. Always check: original to code or code to original? Write arrows!

subsections.title: Forgetting Wrap Around
subsections.content: Z+1 = A (not Z). A-1 = Z (not A). Use circular alphabet: after Z comes A, before A is Z.

subsections.title: Miscounting Positions
subsections.content: Use EJOTY anchors or write positions. Quick check: A=1, E=5, I=9, O=15, U=21.

subsections.title: Assuming Uniform Pattern
subsections.content: Not all codes use same shift. Check 2-3 pairs. Look for vowel/consonant distinction or position-based rules.

subsections.title: Skipping Verification
subsections.content: Take 5 seconds to verify one letter pair. If the code matches your rule, you are correct. One quick check saves 5 wrong answers.

readingSections.title: Quick Revision
readingSections.content: One-page recap of everything for Coding-Decoding.

subsections.title: 30-Second Revision
subsections.content: Forward = Add. Backward = Subtract. Mirror = 27-P. Position = A=1 to Z=26. Substitution = Direct mapping. Always check 2+ pairs.

subsections.title: Interview Tips
subsections.content: This is one of the fastest-scoring topics. Most questions solve in 20-30 seconds with practice. Focus on: speed position finding (EJOTY), pattern detection (check 2 letters), and careful arithmetic (wrap-around). Practice 20 daily questions for automaticity.

formulas.title: Alphabet Position Value
formulas.explanation: Foundation of all coding-decoding. Each letter has a fixed position. Memorize using EJOTY trick: E=5, J=10, O=15, T=20, Y=25.

formulas.title: Forward Letter Shift
formulas.explanation: Each letter shifts forward by a fixed number. Same shift for all or alternating.
formulas.example: CAT -> FDW (+3 each): C+3=F, A+3=D, T+3=W

formulas.title: Backward Letter Shift
formulas.explanation: Each letter shifts backward. Handle wrapping: A-1 = Z.
formulas.example: DOG -> ALD (-3 each): D-3=A, O-3=L, G-3=D

formulas.title: Reverse/Mirror Coding
formulas.explanation: A(1) -> Z(26), B(2) -> Y(25). Just subtract from 27.

formulas.title: Direct Substitution Coding
formulas.explanation: Each letter maps to a specific code letter. May be random but consistent.
formulas.example: If EARTH=QWERT then E->Q, A->W, R->E, T->R, H->T

formulas.title: Mixed Letter-Number Coding
formulas.explanation: Write position number of each letter and join. CAT -> C(3)A(1)T(20) -> 3120.

formulas.title: Symbol to Value Coding
formulas.explanation: Symbols (like stars, circles, #, $) are assigned values.

formulas.title: EJOTY Quick Reference
formulas.explanation: Use these 5 anchor points for quick position finding.
formulas.example: Find M: M is 2 before O(15) -> 13. Find R: R is 3 after O(15) -> 18

Step 2: M->N, O->P, B->C, I->J, L->M, E->F.
